refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the ability to measure/report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The latter is a performance constraint on the system's operation, not a functional requirement of the system itself. The refined FR focuses on the functional capability—providing visibility into energy efficiency—which is solution-neutral and measurable. Achievement of the energy_per_ton ≤ E_target acceptance criterion is a system-level performance requirement that emerges from correct design of propulsion, hydraulics, and operational control DPs, not from a single FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution can collect energy consumption data (fuel/electrical input) and work output metrics (tonnage moved, distance traveled, or equivalent), compute energy_per_ton, and report the result to the Site Manager and System Engineer. Verify over the agreed evaluation period that reported values are traceable to sensor data and calculation logic.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall measure total energy consumed during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall measure work output (tonnage moved or equivalent unit) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute energy efficiency ratio (energy_per_ton) from measured inputs.
FR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall report energy efficiency metrics to authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, System Engineer).

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio offline post-evaluation period
DP2: Deploy real-time energy and tonnage sensors with continuous data aggregation; compute energy_per_ton at configurable intervals; expose metric via reporting interface

DP3: Integrate energy metering into power distribution subsystem; integrate tonnage measurement into load/transport subsystem; synchronize timestamps and compute ratio in a dedicated metering logic module

DP4: Establish energy consumption baseline from historical operational data; measure incremental energy use and tonnage per shift; normalize against baseline to report efficiency delta rather than absolute ratio
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Integrate energy metering into power distribution subsystem; integrate tonnage measurement into load/transport subsystem; synchronize timestamps and compute ratio in a dedicated metering logic module

potential_sources: Timestamp synchronization between energy and tonnage sensors—requires clock discipline or event-driven correlation; mitigate via shared time reference (GPS, NTP, or local sync protocol)

Energy metering placement—if coupled to specific power architecture (AC/DC, voltage level), may limit portability across worksites; mitigate by defining metering interface abstractly (energy joules per interval)

Tonnage measurement dependency on transport method—different worksites may use different hauling equipment; mitigate by defining tonnage as a logical input (mass units per event) independent of sensor type

Reporting interface coupling—if tightly bound to specific data format or protocol, limits integration with external systems; mitigate by defining reporting as a logical contract (metric + timestamp + confidence)
coupling_check.notes: DP3 achieves loose coupling by separating concerns: energy subsystem owns metering, transport subsystem owns tonnage measurement, and a dedicated metering logic module owns synchronization and ratio computation. This avoids monolithic design (DP2) and deferred computation (DP1). DP4 introduces baseline dependency that may obscure absolute performance and complicates acceptance criteria validation.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Energy source is measurable at a single point (e.g., fuel tank, electrical meter). Risk: Distributed or hybrid energy sources (solar + grid + battery) may require multi-point metering; mitigation: define energy metering as a logical aggregation interface.

Assumption: Tonnage is measured at point of delivery or load event. Risk: Partial loads, spillage, or measurement error may inflate or deflate reported tonnage; mitigation: establish measurement accuracy requirement and validation protocol as part of acceptance criteria.

Assumption: Evaluation period is contiguous and well-defined. Risk: Operational downtime, maintenance windows, or sensor failures may create gaps; mitigation: define data quality thresholds and exclusion rules in reporting logic.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan permits installation of metering infrastructure without operational disruption. Risk: Sensor placement, wiring, or calibration may conflict with existing workflows; mitigation: engage operations team early in IM design phase.

Assumption: Timestamp synchronization is achievable within acceptable tolerance (e.g., ±1 second). Risk: Wireless or intermittent connectivity may degrade sync quality; mitigation: implement local buffering and post-hoc correlation in metering logic module.

Assumption: Energy_per_ton metric is sufficient to validate acceptance criteria. Risk: Variability in terrain, load composition, or operator skill may mask systemic inefficiency; mitigation: consider secondary metrics (energy per ton-km, energy per shift) in reporting interface.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable continuous measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output (energy_per_ton = total_energy_joules / total_tonnage_moved) over a defined evaluation period, with data quality assurance and timestamp synchronization between energy and tonnage subsystems, such that the computed metric satisfies energy_per_ton ≤ E_target and supports post-evaluation analysis and acceptance validation. DP3 should be decomposed into three child DPs: (1) DP3a: Measure energy input at power distribution interface and expose as joules per time interval; (2) DP3b: Measure work output (tonnage) at load event and expose as mass units per event; (3) DP3c: Synchronize energy and tonnage streams via shared time reference and compute ratio in a dedicated metering logic module with configurable reporting cadence. This decomposition clarifies module boundaries and reduces coupling between energy and transport subsystems.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized metering logic module that integrates energy metering from power distribution subsystem, tonnage measurement from load/transport subsystem, synchronizes timestamps, and computes efficiency ratios

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Exports computed energy-to-tonnage ratios and metering metrics to external monitoring/analytics systems

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives metering configuration and synchronization parameters from task scheduler

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicates with Vehicle subsystem to retrieve real-time energy and tonnage data; may also receive remote metering directives

module_containment_scores.notes: Strong containment: NGHE Solution is the logical home for cross-subsystem metering orchestration and ratio computation. Energy and tonnage data flow in via Network Link; synchronized metrics flow out via Telemetry. Minor diffusion: Vehicle subsystem must expose energy and tonnage endpoints; power distribution and load/transport subsystems must provide raw sensor feeds.

Weak containment: Vehicle is a platform, not a metering orchestrator. Placing metering logic here couples the vehicle to external analytics and creates tight binding to power and load subsystems. Ratio computation logic would be isolated from the systems it measures, requiring bidirectional data flows and external dependencies.

Poor containment: Teleoperation is a control/interface layer, not a data aggregation or analytics module. Metering logic here would be orthogonal to teleoperation concerns, creating high coupling to Vehicle, power distribution, and load/transport subsystems without clear ownership semantics.

Poor containment: Safety Services is domain-specific to safety-critical functions. Metering and efficiency computation are operational analytics, not safety functions. Placing metering logic here violates separation of concerns and creates unnecessary coupling between safety and performance monitoring.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate boundary because it is the system-level orchestrator responsible for integrating heterogeneous subsystem data (power distribution, load/transport) and computing derived metrics (efficiency ratios). This placement centralizes metering logic, minimizes diffusion of timestamp synchronization and ratio computation across multiple modules, and allows clean external interfaces (Telemetry for output, Network Link for data ingestion, Tasking for configuration).
containment_risks: Risk: Vehicle subsystem must expose energy and tonnage data endpoints; if these are not already available, Vehicle module boundary may need revision to include sensor abstraction layers.

Risk: Timestamp synchronization across distributed subsystems (power distribution, load/transport, Vehicle) may require additional clock-sync or message-ordering logic not fully contained within NGHE Solution; consider whether a dedicated synchronization service is needed.
Risk: If power distribution and load/transport subsystems are tightly coupled to Vehicle, data extraction may require Vehicle to act as a data broker, increasing Vehicle module coupling.

Risk: Real-time metering computation may impose latency constraints on Network Link; if latency is critical, consider whether metering logic should be partially replicated in Vehicle for local computation.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: DP3 is well-aligned with NGHE Solution containment. No revision recommended. However, to further reduce diffusion, explicitly define: (1) a Metering Data Contract interface that Vehicle, power distribution, and load/transport subsystems must implement (energy rate, tonnage, timestamp); (2) a Metering Configuration interface via Tasking to allow external tuning of synchronization windows and ratio thresholds; (3) a Metering Metrics interface via Telemetry for output. This clarifies module boundaries and reduces implicit coupling.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 directly addresses the FR by specifying a complete solution architecture: (1) energy metering integration into power distribution subsystem, (2) tonnage measurement integration into load/transport subsystem, (3) timestamp synchronization and ratio computation in a dedicated metering logic module. The DP is logically sound and traces to the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved ≤ E_target). No logical gaps detected.

diffusion_risks: Vehicle subsystem must expose energy and tonnage data endpoints; if these abstractions do not already exist, Vehicle module boundary may require revision to include sensor data broker or adapter layer, creating secondary diffusion.

Timestamp synchronization logic is distributed across three subsystems (power distribution, load/transport, Vehicle) with synchronization orchestrated in NGHE Solution; if clock skew or message ordering becomes critical, a dedicated synchronization service may be needed, fragmenting the DP across module boundaries.

Power distribution and load/transport subsystems must provide raw sensor feeds to NGHE Solution; if these subsystems are tightly coupled to Vehicle, data extraction may require Vehicle to act as a data broker, increasing Vehicle module coupling and creating indirect diffusion.

Real-time metering computation in NGHE Solution may impose latency constraints on Network Link; if latency SLAs are tight, metering logic may need partial replication in Vehicle for local computation, violating single-responsibility principle and creating DP diffusion.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves strong containment (0.85 score) as the logical home for cross-subsystem metering orchestration and ratio computation. The module boundary is well-defined with clear external interfaces (Telemetry for output, Tasking for configuration, Network Link for data ingestion). Minor diffusion arises from dependency on Vehicle, power distribution, and load/transport subsystems to expose required data endpoints. Recommended mitigation: explicitly define a Metering Data Contract interface that all source subsystems must implement, reducing implicit coupling and clarifying ownership boundaries.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is directly measurable from NGHE Solution outputs. Energy and tonnage data are sourced from well-defined subsystems (power distribution, load/transport); timestamp synchronization is orchestrated within NGHE Solution. Verification requires: (1) validation that energy metering and tonnage measurement are accurate at source, (2) confirmation that timestamp synchronization meets required precision, (3) confirmation that ratio computation logic is correct. All are testable within the NGHE Solution module boundary.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is allocated to NGHE Solution as the primary owner, with supporting responsibilities distributed to Vehicle (data broker), power distribution (energy metering), and load/transport (tonnage measurement). Allocation is traceable: FR → DP3 (metering architecture) → IM (NGHE Solution + supporting subsystem interfaces). However, allocation clarity could be strengthened by explicitly documenting: (1) which subsystem owns the Metering Data Contract interface, (2) which subsystem owns timestamp synchronization logic (NGHE Solution or a dedicated service), (3) latency and precision SLAs for each data source.

recommended_revisions: Define a formal Metering Data Contract interface that power distribution, load/transport, and Vehicle subsystems must implement. This contract should specify: energy rate (W or kWh), tonnage (kg or tons), timestamp (UTC with precision), and data freshness guarantees. This reduces implicit coupling and clarifies module boundaries.

Explicitly assign ownership of timestamp synchronization logic. If NGHE Solution owns it, document the synchronization algorithm (e.g., NTP, message ordering, clock skew tolerance). If a dedicated synchronization service is needed, create a new IM boundary and update DP3 to reference it.

Define a Metering Configuration interface via Tasking to allow external tuning of: synchronization windows, ratio thresholds, metering frequency, and alert conditions. This clarifies the contract between Tasking and NGHE Solution and reduces ad-hoc coupling.

Define a Metering Metrics interface via Telemetry to specify output schema: energy_per_ton, energy_total, tonnage_total, measurement_period, timestamp, quality_flags. This clarifies the contract between NGHE Solution and external monitoring/analytics systems.

Document latency and precision SLAs for each data source (energy, tonnage, timestamp). If real-time metering computation imposes tight latency constraints on Network Link, evaluate whether partial metering logic replication in Vehicle is justified; if so, update DP3 and create a secondary IM boundary for Vehicle-local metering.

Clarify whether Vehicle subsystem already exposes energy and tonnage data endpoints, or whether Vehicle module boundary must be revised to include sensor abstraction layers. If revision is needed, update IM boundary and document the impact on Vehicle module containment.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. However, the gate results expose critical diffusion risks and allocation clarity gaps that must be resolved through explicit sub-FR decomposition before proceeding to detailed design. The recommended revisions—particularly the Metering Data Contract, timestamp synchronization ownership, and latency SLA definition—are architectural decisions that require functional decomposition to formalize. Additionally, safety criticality (high) demands that metering accuracy, data freshness, and synchronization precision be explicitly captured as sub-FRs with measurable acceptance criteria. Decomposing now prevents downstream rework and clarifies module boundaries for Vehicle, power distribution, and load/transport subsystems.
next_level_sub_frs: FR3.1: Establish and enforce a Metering Data Contract interface that power distribution, load/transport, and Vehicle subsystems must implement, specifying energy rate (W or kWh), tonnage (kg or tons), timestamp (UTC with precision), and data freshness guarantees.

FR3.2: Synchronize timestamps across power distribution, load/transport, and Vehicle subsystems with precision ≤ [TBD: e.g., 100 ms] and clock skew tolerance ≤ [TBD: e.g., 50 ms], ensuring ratio computation (energy_per_ton) is based on temporally aligned measurements.
FR3.3: Compute energy-per-ton ratio (energy_used / ton_moved) with measurement accuracy ≥ [TBD: e.g., 95%] and latency ≤ [TBD: e.g., 5 seconds] to support real-time metering and alerting.

FR3.4: Validate that energy metering at source (power distribution subsystem) meets accuracy ≤ [TBD: e.g., ±2%] and that tonnage measurement at source (load/transport subsystem) meets accuracy ≤ [TBD: e.g., ±1%], ensuring end-to-end ratio computation fidelity.

FR3.5: Define and expose a Metering Configuration interface via Tasking to allow external tuning of synchronization windows, ratio thresholds, metering frequency, and alert conditions without modifying NGHE Solution core logic.

FR3.6: Define and expose a Metering Metrics interface via Telemetry with output schema (energy_per_ton, energy_total, tonnage_total, measurement_period, timestamp, quality_flags) to enable external monitoring, analytics, and compliance reporting.

FR3.7: Clarify Vehicle subsystem data broker responsibilities: determine whether Vehicle already exposes energy and tonnage data endpoints, or whether Vehicle module boundary must be revised to include sensor abstraction layers (e.g., sensor adapter, data broker service).

FR3.8: Evaluate latency and precision trade-offs: if real-time metering computation imposes tight latency constraints on Network Link (< [TBD: e.g., 1 second]), determine whether partial metering logic replication in Vehicle is justified; if so, define replication scope and synchronization protocol to maintain single-responsibility principle.

revise.why: DP3 is logically sound but lacks explicit specification of: (1) Metering Data Contract interface definition and ownership, (2) timestamp synchronization algorithm and ownership (NGHE Solution vs. dedicated service), (3) latency and precision SLAs for each data source, (4) Vehicle data broker responsibilities and module boundary implications, (5) Metering Configuration and Metering Metrics interface schemas. These are architectural decisions that must be formalized in DP before IM boundaries can be finalized. Revise DP3 to include these specifications as sub-DPs, or decompose FR3 into sub-FRs that each address one of these concerns. This ensures that module boundaries (IM) are explicit and that diffusion risks are mitigated at the design level, not discovered during implementation.

assumptions: Power distribution and load/transport subsystems can expose raw sensor feeds (energy rate, tonnage) with defined precision and freshness guarantees.
Vehicle subsystem can act as a data broker or already exposes required data endpoints; if not, Vehicle module boundary revision is acceptable.
Timestamp synchronization precision requirement is ≤ 100 ms (TBD: to be confirmed with safety and metering accuracy analysis).
Energy metering accuracy at source is ≤ ±2% and tonnage measurement accuracy is ≤ ±1% (TBD: to be confirmed with metrology and safety requirements).
Real-time metering computation latency requirement is ≤ 5 seconds (TBD: to be confirmed with operational and alerting requirements).
NGHE Solution is the logical owner of cross-subsystem metering orchestration and ratio computation; timestamp synchronization logic ownership (NGHE Solution vs. dedicated service) is TBD.
Network Link can support metering data ingestion without violating existing latency SLAs; if not, partial metering logic replication in Vehicle is acceptable.
open_risks: Timestamp synchronization across distributed subsystems may require a dedicated synchronization service, fragmenting DP3 across module boundaries and increasing complexity.
Vehicle subsystem may not currently expose energy and tonnage data endpoints; if Vehicle module boundary revision is needed, impact on Vehicle containment and coupling must be re-evaluated.

Real-time metering computation may impose latency constraints on Network Link that conflict with existing SLAs; if so, partial metering logic replication in Vehicle may be necessary, violating single-responsibility principle.
Power distribution and load/transport subsystems may not implement Metering Data Contract interface consistently, leading to data quality issues and ratio computation errors.
Clock skew or message ordering issues in timestamp synchronization may cause ratio computation inaccuracy, particularly for high-speed or high-variability operations.
Safety criticality (high) requires that metering accuracy, data freshness, and synchronization precision be validated end-to-end; validation strategy and acceptance criteria are not yet defined.
evidence_needed: Confirmation of timestamp synchronization precision requirement (e.g., ≤ 100 ms) based on metering accuracy and operational needs.
Confirmation of energy metering accuracy (e.g., ≤ ±2%) and tonnage measurement accuracy (e.g., ≤ ±1%) from power distribution and load/transport subsystem specifications.
Confirmation of real-time metering computation latency requirement (e.g., ≤ 5 seconds) based on alerting and operational needs.
Audit of Vehicle subsystem to determine whether energy and tonnage data endpoints already exist; if not, estimate effort and impact of Vehicle module boundary revision.
Audit of power distribution and load/transport subsystems to determine whether they can implement Metering Data Contract interface; if not, identify gaps and mitigation strategies.
Definition of timestamp synchronization algorithm (e.g., NTP, message ordering, clock skew tolerance) and ownership (NGHE Solution vs. dedicated service).
Definition of Metering Configuration and Metering Metrics interface schemas and ownership.
Latency and precision SLA analysis for Network Link to determine whether partial metering logic replication in Vehicle is justified.
Safety and compliance analysis to confirm that metering accuracy, data freshness, and synchronization precision meet regulatory and operational requirements.
